description: You are a QA engineer analysing two application log files that already exist in /home/user/logs/. Each log line contains a timestamp, the string “ERROR”, and then an alphanumeric error-code that always begins with the capital letter “E” followed by exactly three digits (e.g. E102). Your goal is to produce a comma-separated file that shows how many times each distinct error-code appears across both log files. 1. Read and combine the contents of /home/user/logs/app1.log and /home/user/logs/app2.log. 2. Isolate every error-code (the token that matches the regular expression E[0-9]\{3\}). 3. Count the occurrences of each distinct code using the standard GNU coreutils workflow of sort → uniq -c (you may add any other shell utilities such as cut or awk as helpers). 4. Sort the final list by • primary key: descending numeric frequency, • secondary key: ascending alphabetical error-code so that a code with a higher count always appears before one with a lower count, and codes with equal counts are in alphabetical order. 5. Write the result to a new file named /home/user/logs/error_frequency.csv. • The file /home/user/logs/error_frequency.csv must contain only the data lines—no header row, no spaces, one record per line, exactly two fields per line: , Example of correct formatting style (values for illustration only): E999,42 E103,17 E007,17 • There must be a trailing newline after the very last line. • The file must be world-readable (mode 644 is fine). • Complete the task exclusively with standard POSIX/GNU utilities available on a typical Ubuntu/Fedora container; do not rely on Python, Perl or custom binaries. • You do not have root privileges. Operate as the normal non-root user. • Do not alter the original log files in any way. • Place the generated error_frequency.csv in the same /home/user/logs/ directory so that automated tests can find it. Automated tests will read /home/user/logs/error_frequency.csv and expect: • correct file path and name, • correct world-readable permissions, • exactly four non-empty lines (in this particular data set), • every line matching the required pattern ^E[0-9]{3},[1-9][0-9]*$, • proper ordering as specified above, • exact frequencies computed from the current contents of app1.log and app2.log. Any deviation will fail the test. A classic pipeline is cat → grep/awk → sort → uniq -c → sort -k1,1nr -k2 followed by a formatter such as awk '{print $2","$1}'. Keep the data flow in one command or build it step-by-step—either is fine as long as the final CSV file meets all requirements.
truth:
<!--
        PRE-EXISTING FILES
    -->
    <initial_files>
        <!-- app1.log -->
        <file path="/home/user/logs/app1.log"><![CDATA[
[2024-06-01 10:00:00] ERROR E102 Connection timeout
[2024-06-01 10:00:01] WARN  W301 Cache miss
[2024-06-01 10:00:02] ERROR E104 Invalid token
[2024-06-01 10:00:03] ERROR E102 Connection timeout
[2024-06-01 10:00:04] ERROR E108 Disk full
[2024-06-01 10:00:05] ERROR E102 Connection timeout
]]></file>

        <!-- app2.log -->
        <file path="/home/user/logs/app2.log"><![CDATA[
2024-06-01T11:00:00Z ERROR E108 Disk full
2024-06-01T11:00:01Z ERROR E102 Connection timeout
2024-06-01T11:00:02Z ERROR E104 Invalid token
2024-06-01T11:00:03Z ERROR E104 Invalid token
2024-06-01T11:00:04Z ERROR E114 Null pointer
2024-06-01T11:00:05Z ERROR E108 Disk full
2024-06-01T11:00:06Z ERROR E104 Invalid token
2024-06-01T11:00:07Z ERROR E114 Null pointer
2024-06-01T11:00:08Z ERROR E102 Connection timeout
2024-06-01T11:00:09Z ERROR E102 Connection timeout
]]></file>
    </initial_files>

    <!--
        EXPECTED FILE CREATED BY AGENT
    -->
    <expected_output>
        <file path="/home/user/logs/error_frequency.csv" mode="0644"><![CDATA[
E102,6
E104,4
E108,3
E114,2
]]></file>
    </expected_output>
